title: Seagrass and rush plaited doormat
subtitle: A plaited doormat from seagrass and rush rope in a herringbone pattern
excerpt: A heavy-duty doormat plaited from seagrass cord and twisted rush rope in a herringbone weave. The finished mat is 500 mm by 350 mm and 15 mm thick. Seagrass cord is available ready-twisted; rush rope is made by twisting soaked soft rush in the hand. The herringbone pattern is set up on a simple peg frame.
sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the plaited mat construction and herringbone pattern; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the household doormat form.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried and twisted cord made from dried seagrass leaves. Sold by weight in cord form; hard-wearing and resistant to moisture.
The stems of soft rush or bulrush, gathered and dried. Twisted in groups of four to six stems to form rope for plaiting.
A zigzag plait pattern where each warp strand passes over two then under two weft strands in offset rows, creating a diagonal V pattern.
Immersing dried rush stems in water to restore flexibility before twisting and plaiting.

content.text: A plaited mat is heavier and more durable than a coiled mat for doorstep use. The
ropes over and under in alternating groups of two, shifted one position on each row. A temporary peg frame keeps the warp strands tensioned and evenly spaced while the weft is woven in.

For a 500 by 350 mm mat: 12 metres of 6 mm seagrass cord for the warp; 300 g of dried rush for the weft rope, or 10 metres of ready-made 6 mm rush cord. The warp strands run along the 500 mm length of the mat; eight warp strands at 50 mm spacing cover the 350 mm width. The sloyd knife is used to trim and taper rope ends flush at the edges.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.

restores flexibility: ten minutes in the trough, then twenty minutes wrapped in a damp cloth. Take groups of five or six stems, hold the butt ends together, and roll them tightly on your thigh to twist them into a rope. Keep the twist continuous from end to end. Two people twisting in opposite directions on each end produces the most even rope. If using ready-made rush cord, skip this step.

Knock eight nails into a board at 50 mm spacing in a straight line. These are the warp pegs. Loop the seagrass warp cords around the pegs so each cord doubles back and produces two parallel strands. Separate the cords into over-under groups of two in preparation for the herringbone weave.

Thread the first rush rope weft strand through the warp: over two, under two, for the full width. Push the row tight to the top of the warp using the bodkin. The second row shifts one position: under one, over two, under two, and so on. Continue alternating rows, shifting the pattern by one each time, to build the
diagonal. Pack each weft row tight with the bodkin before adding the next.

When the mat is 350 mm long, remove it from the peg frame. Thread each warp cord end back through the last two rows of weaving using the bodkin to lock it. Trim the ends flush with the sloyd knife. Tuck any protruding rush weft ends under the edge rows and secure with a lashing stitch.

attrs.symptom: The pattern breaks down after a few rows
attrs.cause: Lost count of the over-under shift
attrs.fix: Count the warp groups from the left edge before each row and check the pattern position. Mark the shift point on each row with a coloured thread temporarily until the rhythm is established.

attrs.symptom: The mat is narrower at one end than the other
attrs.cause: Warp cords pulled inward by uneven weft tension
attrs.fix: Tie a temporary tie-cord across the full width of the warp at the start of each weaving session to hold the spacing. Remove the tie-cord as the last row reaches its position.
